title: 控方证人
summary:
Charles Richewright believes his illegal schemes to secure wealth from his deceased aunt, Mrs. Hart, remain hidden. However, his confidence is shaken when Mr. Hopkinson, Mrs. Hart's long-time legal advisor, arrives to discuss her will. Hopkinson reveals that Mrs. Hart had requested the will be sent to him the previous Tuesday, but the document is currently missing.

After searching Mrs. Hart's documents and finding nothing, Charles summons the maid, Elizabeth, for assistance. Elizabeth testifies that she saw Mrs. Hart holding a will in a long blue envelope on the morning of her death, and she found an empty blue envelope on the table the following morning. Charles produces this empty envelope for Hopkinson. When Hopkinson asks if the fireplace has been burning, Elizabeth confirms it has been lit continuously.

Hopkinson suggests that Mrs. Hart may have instructed him to destroy the will, implying a possible conflict between her and Charles. Although Charles denies any friction with his aunt, Hopkinson remains skeptical. Charles suddenly realizes the will may have accidentally fallen into the fireplace ashes during his aunt's death. Hopkinson informs him that if the current will is not found, a previous version from September 1920 remains valid, which leaves the entire estate to Mrs. Hart's niece, Miriam Robinson.

A telephone call from a doctor reveals that Mrs. Hart's heart condition was so severe she had only two months to live, a fact that offers Charles no comfort; instead, he feels the impending shadow of imprisonment.

In a separate scene, a lawyer named Mr. Merriweather meets with his client, Leonard Walcot, who is on trial for murder. Despite Merriweather's assurances that they will clear Walcot's name, the young man remains in despair, and Merriweather privately doubts the strength of their defense.
